verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I beg you, my most sweet friend, who are so curious that you even know my dreams, and that you scrutinize for purposes of accusations all that I have written during these many years without fear of future calumny; answer me, how is it you do not know the prefaces of the very books on which you ground your charges against me?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: These prefaces, as if by some prophetic foresight, gave the answer to the calumnies that were coming, thus fulfilling the proverb, “The antidote before the poison.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What harm has been done to the churches by my translation?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You bought up, as I knew, at great cost the versions of Aquila, Symmachus, and Theodotion, and the Jewish authors of the fifth and sixth translations.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Your Origen, or, that I may not seem to be wounding you with fictitious praises, our Origen, (for I may call him ours for his genius and learning, though not for the truth of his doctrines) in all his books explains and expounds not only the Septuagint but the Jewish versions.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Eusebius and Didymus do the same.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I do not mention Apollinarius, who, with a laudable zeal though not according to knowledge, attempted to patch up into one garment the rags of all the translations, and to weave a consistent text of Scripture at his own discretion, not according to any sound rule of criticism.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The Hebrew Scriptures are used by apostolic men; they are used, as is evident, by the apostles and evangelists.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Our Lord and Saviour himself whenever he refers to the Scriptures, takes his quotations from the Hebrew; as in the instance of the words “He that believeth on me, as the Scripture hath said, out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water,” and in the words used on the cross itself, “Eli, Eli, lama sabachthani,” which is by interpretation “My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?” not, as it is given by the Septuagint, “My God, my God, look upon me, why hast thou forsaken me?” and many similar cases.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I do not say this in order to aim a blow at the seventy translators; but I assert that the Apostles of Christ have an authority superior to theirs.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Wherever the Seventy agree with the Hebrew, the apostles took their quotations from that translation; but, where they disagree, they set down in Greek what they had found in the Hebrew.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And further, I give a challenge to my accuser.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I have shown that many things are set down in the New Testament as coming from the older books, which are not to be found in the Septuagint; and I have pointed out that these exist in the Hebrew.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Now let him show that there is anything in the New Testament which comes from the Septuagint but which is not found in the Hebrew, and our controversy is at an end.
